quote: it is indisputable that our universe is not chaos. We perceive beings, objects, things to which we give names. These beings or things are forms or structures endowed with a degree of stability; they take up some part of space and last for some period of time.
author: René Thom

quote: the universe we see is a ceaseless creation, evolution, and destruction of forms and the purpose of science is to foresee this change of form and, if possible, explain it.
author: René Thom

quote: The concept of structural stability seems to me to be a key idea in the interpretation of phenomena at all branches of science… forms that are subjectively identifiable and are represented in our language by a substantive are necessarily structurally stable forms: any given object is always under the disturbing influence of its environment, and these influences, however slight, will have some effect on its form.
author: René Thom

quote: the hypothesis of structural stability of isolated scientific processes is implicit in all scientific observation.
author: René Thom

quote: all the basic intuitive ideas of morphogenesis can be found in Heraclitus: all that I have done is to place these in a geometric and dynamic framework that will make them some day accessible to quantitative analysis. The 'solemn, unadorned words,' like those of the sibyl that have sounded without faltering throughout the centuries, deserve this distant echo.
author: René Thom

quote: the degree of interest or importance of a mathematical or physical idea rarely corresponds to its formal rigor
author: René Thom

quote: usually, when we speak of 'information,' we should use the word 'form.'
author: René Thom

quote: from this point of view the fundamental problem of biology is a topological one, for topology is precisely the mathematical discipline dealing with the passage from the local to the global
author: René Thom

quote: It was only quite recently, almost by chance, that I discovered the work of Aristotle. It was fascinating reading, almost from the start.
author: René Thom

quote: The philosophical program I had in mind for Catastrophe Theory, namely the geometrization of thought and linguistic activity, was more than merely sketched out in Aristotle, it was already largely achieved.
author: René Thom

quote: For hundreds, maybe thousands, of years, Aristotle has been the only one to think in terms of the continuous. To my mind, that is his chief merit.
author: René Thom

quote: in him we find a philosophy that is both materialist (existence requiring a material substrate) and nevertheless governed by form and final causes. An association rarely encountered in modern times.
author: René Thom

quote: This work is rather intended to take up the succession of a dead discipline, namely 'natural philosophy'.
author: René Thom

quote: We will not be looking for the foundation of geometry in logic. On the contrary, we shall be seeing logic as a derived activity (a secondary one, when all is said and done, in the history of the human mind), a rhetoric.
author: René Thom

quote: Instead of building geometry in a logical manner, we will seek to base what is logical on geometry.
author: René Thom

quote: I wanted to describe here something that might be called 'protophysics', source and reservoir of all permanent intuition, of all those archetypal metaphors that have nourished man's imagination over the ages.
author: René Thom

quote: The primary experience in any receiving of phenomena is discontinuity. But discontinuity presupposes the continuous.
author: René Thom

quote: The ideal of contemporary science—and of Positivism—is to reduce everything to salient forms, with no interaction allowed other than collision between salient forms, and to do away entirely with pregnance. It manages to do so (as in quantum mechanics) only by foregoing intelligibility.
author: René Thom

quote: Physicists have been led to adopt what I call a 'demiurgic' attitude. One imagines the world to have been constructed by an intelligent Demiurge with the help of a few simple formulas. The aim of science is to find out what these formulas are and allow man to realize the Promethean dream of ruling the world.
author: René Thom

quote: Pragmatism, in a way, is hardly more than the conceptualized form of a certain return to animal nature.
author: René Thom

quote: Only some realist metaphysics can give back meaning to this world of ours.
author: René Thom

quote: What is so interesting about Aristotelianism is that it is a philosophy both materialist (inasmuch as it denies the possibility of a Platonic existence without matter), and finalist. An association which, we have said, has not been found since.
author: René Thom

quote: To understand means, above all, to geometrize.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition; verify against Italian/French original

quote: Descartes, with his vortices and hooked atoms, explained everything and calculated nothing: Newton, with the law of gravitation in 1/r², calculated everything and explained nothing.
author: René Thom
note: Thom cites this as a quotation from his own SSM; translated from Spanish

quote: I prefer the field of mathematics where one does not know too well what one is doing!
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: Bourbaki has embalmed mathematics, reduced it, so to speak, to a mummy!
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: Explanation and prediction are fairly opposed objectives in the scientific enterprise!
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: Between these two fundamental aims of science — understanding the world and acting upon it — I believe it would be better to subordinate the second to the first, rather than the first to the second.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: Catastrophe theory is not a scientific theory in the ordinary sense of the term… I would say rather that it is a methodology, or perhaps a kind of language, that allows the data of experience to be organized under the most varied conditions.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: I will repeat one of my favorite phrases: a morphology arises from the conflict of two (or more) attractors.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: in no case does mathematics have the right to dictate anything to reality.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: what limits the true is not the false, but the insignificant. And modern science, at the point where it finds itself, is a veritable torrent of insignificance.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: Either the analogy is true, and then it is sterile; or it is bold, and then it may be fruitful. Only by running the risk of error can one arrive at what is new.
author: René Thom
note: Translated from Spanish edition

quote: We have rediscovered Heraclitus.
author: René Thom
note: Closing sentence of the book; translated from Spanish edition

quote: At a time where so many scholars are calculating, is it not desirable that some, who can, dream?
author: René Thom
note: Source unconfirmed; attributed to SSM on Goodreads

quote: There seems to be a time scale in all natural processes beyond which structural stability and calculability become incompatible. In planetary mechanics this scale is of such an extent that the incompatibility is not evident, whereas in quantum mechanics it is so short that the incompatibility is immediately felt, and today the physicist sacrifices structural stability for computability. I hope that he will not have cause to regret this choice.
author: René Thom
note: Source unconfirmed; attributed to SSM on Goodreads

quote: The essential point of view advanced here is this: the stability of every living being, as of every structurally stable form, rests, in the last analysis, on a formal structure—in fact, a geometrical object—whose biochemical realization is the living being.
author: René Thom
note: Source unconfirmed; attributed to Morphogenèse et imaginaire on Goodreads

quote: There is hardly any doubt that, from a psychological and, for the writer, ontological point of view, the geometric continuum is the primordial entity. If one has any consciousness at all, it is consciousness of time and space; geometric continuity is in some way inseparably bound to conscious thought.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes or Semiophysics; unverified

quote: The constructive and operational aspect of mathematics is necessarily linked to its discrete aspect, that is, to discontinuity. Since the operations cannot be carried out simultaneously, each of them takes place in a well-defined segment of time. The indefinite iteration of operations creates objects for which it is often difficult, if not impossible, to obtain intuitive representations.
author: René Thom
note: Unverified; circulates on AZQuotes/LibQuotes without source

quote: A continuous space given in the intuition always has some further structure, a complement so to speak, of properties; a topology, a metrics, a dimension… They are complementary data which more often than not allow the operations and constructions to be defined.
author: René Thom
note: Unverified; circulates on AZQuotes/LibQuotes without source

quote: If one must choose between rigour and meaning, I shall unhesitatingly choose the latter.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes; unverified

quote: Catastrophe Theory is—quite likely—the first coherent attempt (since Aristotelian logic) to give a theory on analogy. When narrow-minded scientists object to Catastrophe Theory that it gives no more than analogies, or metaphors, they do not realise that they are stating the proper aim of Catastrophe Theory, which is to classify all possible types of analogous situations.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes or Semiophysics; unverified

quote: The world of ideas is not revealed to us in one stroke; we must both permanently and unceasingly recreate it in our consciousness.
author: René Thom
note: Unverified; circulates on AZQuotes without source

quote: All models divide naturally into two a priori parts: one is kinematics, whose aim is to parameterize the forms of the states of the process under consideration, and the other is dynamics, describing the evolution in time of these forms.
author: René Thom
note: Attributed to SSM on Goodreads; page unconfirmed

quote: I believe that proving is not a natural activity for mathematicians.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes; unverified

quote: The purely formal language of geometry describes adequately the reality of space. We might say, in this sense, that geometry is successful magic. I should like to state a converse: is not all magic, to the extent that it is successful, geometry?
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes; unverified

quote: Algebra is rich in structure but weak in meaning.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes; unverified

quote: Quantum mechanics, with its leap into statistics, has been a mere palliative for our ignorance.
author: René Thom
note: Attributed to SSM on AZQuotes; page unconfirmed

quote: The importance of the 'New Mathematics' lies mainly in the fact that it has taught us the difference between the disc and the circle.
author: René Thom
note: Likely Paraboles et catastrophes; unverified

quote: If we admit a priori that science is just acquisition of knowledge, that is, building an inventory of all observable phenomena in a given disciplinary domain — then, obviously, any science is empirical.
author: René Thom
note: LibQuotes lists as sourced; source title not displayed

quote: The spirit of geometry circulates almost everywhere in the immense body of mathematics, and it is a major pedagogical error to seek to eliminate it.
author: René Thom
note: LibQuotes lists as sourced; source title not displayed

quote: Everything considered, mathematicians should have the courage of their most profound convictions and thus affirm that mathematical forms indeed have an existence that is independent of the mind considering them… Yet, at any given moment, mathematicians have only an incomplete and fragmentary view of this world of ideas.
author: René Thom
note: LibQuotes lists as sourced; source title not displayed

quote: Topology is precisely the mathematical discipline that allows the passage from local to global.
author: René Thom
note: Shorter standalone variant of the p.177 SSM passage; page unconfirmed
